narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—64.5% chance of not passing, which tipped the scales against admission. Picture it like a story the model pieced together from your profile: at the top was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, the strongest signal pulling toward failure, showing your grades placed you at the bottom of your class when it mattered most for building legal skills. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 26, another heavy anchor dragging the odds down, as it suggested challenges with the kind of analytical reasoning the bar demands. Your Year 1 decile of 1.0 reinforced this early pattern, painting a picture of consistent struggles in a competitive academic environment. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, surprisingly nudged toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps because it didn't signal the extra resources that sometimes help others push through. Your background as a Black male applicant added another layer pulling in that direction, reflecting broader patterns the model has seen in bar passage rates. Your undergrad GPA of 3.1 and full-time student status continued this trend, each contributing their weight to the story of hurdles ahead. The one bright note was your gender, which slightly tipped toward success, like a small tailwind in a headwind storm. It's this combination—low deciles dominating the narrative—that made the model worry your profile matches too many who faltered on the bar despite their determination. You're clearly driven, aiming straight for law with that full-time commitment, and this isn't the end of your story; many have climbed from similar starting points with targeted improvements like LSAT prep or grade boosts. Hang in there—we see your potential, even if the numbers right now tell a cautious tale.
